speaker: interviewee
text: --on the whole end. When I'm through, then we can talk, in general, about the family. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there something-- reflection?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, I was getting little pinspot reflections coming from that mirror there.

speaker: interviewee
text: The mirrors?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. I might be able to fix that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Maybe at the end-- the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were telling me when you went back there--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Sig Halbreich-- a continuation of the interview. And it's the 12th of July. OK. Here we go.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Sig, you were telling us, at this point, that you were on the Yugoslav border, and you had just been turned over by a Croat--

speaker: interviewee
text: Croat soldier.

speaker: interviewer
text: --soldier to the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the Germans-- on the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the border.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was the end of basically--

speaker: interviewee
text: My liberty.

speaker: interviewer
text: --your liberation. And this was what-- what date?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was about October 19th, 20th-- yeah. Because we left 17th home, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the year-- what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah-- '39, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell us then what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: He handed us over to the Germans. They arrested us. And after a couple of days being in Marburg, in jail, they transferred us to Graz. And to there, I remained about 10 days. In the meantime, they had found out from my hometown who we are. We are not only Polish officers. But we are Jews and very well-known active Zionists. Though, first of all, they took me, put me on the train-- with a guard, naturally-- to Vienna-- in Vienna, overnight in jail. And afternoon, they put me again on the station in a train to Berlin. We didn't know where we go. I was in the car with about 20, 25, maybe say, 27 people-- civilian people from Vienna, traveling with me in my compartment-- and fairly good-looking, well-dressed people with ties and-- but not one word. During all the time, we never exchanged a word. And I don't think that they knew where they are going. I, for sure, did not know. And we finally, the next morning-- we traveled all night from Vienna to Berlin-- the next morning, we went out of the train. And there were already trucks waiting for us. We were loaded on the trucks, until we were riding about maybe an hour. And we got out of the trucks. And here in front of us, a huge gate. And written on top, 'Arbeit Macht Frei.' It means labor makes you free. Still, we didn't know where we are. We were let through the gate, and they separated me from all other ones. And we were standing on a huge place. It was a roll-call place.

speaker: interviewee
text: And for-- after a while, SS Men-- soldiers coming towards this group and starting to hit them, beat them up, call them names. And I just couldn't understand. What's going on here?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, the beatings, they turn only to them and beat them up. And they let me free. I'm standing. I couldn't explain what it meant. After we-- I was already at the camp. I found out they were homosexuals-- high officials of the Austrian government. They were arrested and brought to the camps. I was standing by myself. A few minutes later, an SS man comes to me. Come with me. I'm going to the office. He takes my identifications, name, from where, and who I am, and so far, and takes me to a shower-- naturally, a cold shower. I had-- they registered my clothes, my money, my papers, everything, and my luggage, naturally, and put me in a new uniform. The prisoner's uniforms, they're blue and gray striped clothes. And he took me to a barrack, and there were already Jews in the barrack. We Jews were naturally separate. It was Sachsenhausen, the first camp, near-- near Berlin. And I came to the barrack. Then there were already Jews there, but Jews from Germany-- Polish Jews from Germany. They were right away, September 1st, arrested and brought to the camps. There were four barracks occupied by Jews. We were about, I would say, 800 to 1,000 Jews. It was very crowded, because normally, a barrack is not-- is maybe occupied by 100 people. But we were 200 and more. They asked me from where I come. And I told them from Poland. And they were surprised. It's already from Poland, in October, '39. They're already-- they're bringing already Jews from Poland. I was the first Pole-- not only Jews-- but the first Pole in a German concentration camp in '39, in October.

speaker: interviewee
text: We ask question, and they answer us. What are you doing here? Nothing. We're just waiting.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the morning, we get up. But in the evening, we go to-- on the straw sacks. We spread out the straw sacks, and we lay down. But this is it. And there was no work for us, but they had a new method. In the morning, when we got up, we got this little-- a container with a hot, black water. They called it coffee, but we called it water, because this was not coffee-- and nothing else.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we were taken out to the roll-call in the morning, in front of the barracks. When we were standing, next to us, we had to put up the bodies of people who died next to the-- to us. When the SS man came, the first thing he asked-- not how many people, but how many dead? He was more interested in the dead people. How many? And when the block eldest gave him the report, so and so many, he said, oh, only 15? That's not enough. Tomorrow, I send. I want to see more. They were only interested to kill.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after he went, we had to sit down in a crouching position this way-- hands behind the neck-- and sit this way. And we couldn't move. But somebody, after hours and hours, people got tired. They wanted to relax, move from one leg to another one. And when SS man noticed it, he ran into the line, beats them up. Sometimes he kills them for this. And this was up till noontime, about 12:00. Then we got up. They gave us a food. The-- they brought a kettle of foods. And about in a 1/2 an hour, we got again-- again a container with soup-- and on the beginning, a fairly nutritious soup-- beans or peas. Friday, we got a fish soup.

speaker: interviewee
text: Once in a while, we found a piece-- a little piece of-- of meat. But it was-- of course, we didn't work. We just were sitting, you know? So we couldn't complain. But the treatments were the worst. What happened? Tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- in the-- at night, even, when we were laying on the straw sacks, they came through the windows with water hoses and with sticks. And they beat us up and stepped on us. And many people were killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they didn't leave us. They didn't let us relax, even. They kept us busy. This was, I would say, the biggest weapon towards us-- the Germans-- to kill us physically and mentally too. This was their strength.

speaker: interviewee
text: And soon, we changed. We turned into robots. Automatically, we did what we have to do in the morning and in every while-- every while. And at night, the same thing. We didn't think. We stopped thinking. Our minds didn't work at all. And this was actually the worst thing that could happen to us. After about three months, they had a new method. It got spring. It got warmer. So we had to run around these four barracks. And-- and every 10, 15 yards, they were standing with a whip, and they were hitting us. And we had to run for hours and hours, all day. And we noticed they had an eye mostly on the old ones. They want to get rid of the old people first. And we younger ones, when we saw it, we wanted to help them. So when we saw he's picking up his arm and want to hit the old one, we run onto it, so we were hit. And we saved him a little longer-- 50s, this old man.

speaker: interviewee
text: But soon, we got more weaker and weaker. And we had to look for our life. It means self-preservation. So we stopped it, you know? And we were hit too.

speaker: interviewee
text: It went on another two months. All of a sudden, to-- to work. We marched from the camp to a empty-- huge, empty field. And there they started to build a red brick factory. And there, marched from the camp to the-- to the factory. We were not allowed to talk to each other when we marched. And we shouldn't-- we were not allowed to turn our heads. Because when we-- somebody turned his head, he was shot under the pretext he wanted to escape. And they did it, because when they shot a prisoner during escape, they got a one-week furlough-- the SS men. Well, it turned out they used it so often, that less and less people left-- remained. So then they got an order to stop it. But you see, sometimes they took a prisoner's cap and throw it on the side-- let's go for it. And when he went for it, boom, boom, boom. He shoots them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day, we marching, and I feel I'm observed-- somebody observing me. But I was afraid to turn. I didn't turn. And slowly, slowly, the SS man comes close to me and says, Jesus-- in-- in-- in Polish. Jesus, Maria, Siggy, you are here? Oh, this fell down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oops.

speaker: interviewee
text: There. I'm changing legs.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know how that is.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, you were telling me about--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I turn to him, and I look. It was a friend from high school. He was a German, and I was a Jew. But we went-- we got along very well-- always. And I said, what are you doing here, George? You know, I'm a German, and they put me to the SS. And I have to serve it. And from this day on, when he was on duty with us, he always left me in a piece-- a piece of bread. And this, again, is part of my survival. A little more meant so much, you know, to stay alive. And it went on a few months. And he disappeared. I don't know if he was transferred, or something. He was gone. During the work-- it's a factory. It's this huge place. We had shovels and picks. And first of all, we did the earth-moving. It was very rough, because it was frozen yet from the winter. And we Jews were always in one group-- never separated. And it was extremely hard to work-- not only the work, but the treatment. They chased us around. And they beat us up, only just to get rid of it as soon as possible. And one day, during lunchtime, I met a German prisoner. He was a communist and was arrested because he was a communist. And he was the kapo of the carpenter division, the carpentry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we got to talk too. He said, tell me, how do you feel? I said, oh, fine. OK. How is it at work? I said, it's very hard, and I don't know how long we will be able to make it. He said, you know, I will try to get you into my division as a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Next morning, we march to the factory. My name was called. I then step out. He said, you go to the carpentry. You will work with the carpentry. He arranged it. I came there. Naturally, it changed everything. There you are not being changed-- touched. You're not being beaten. You work on the-- on the roof. You're protected from the climate-- from the rain, from the snow, from the sun. And nobody bothers you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Once in a while, SS man comes, looks at what's going on. But you are not guarded, see? And I learned very fast carpentry-- how to use a nail-- a hammer, and a saw, and all those things, you know? And this went on for two years.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then in 1941, in September-- yeah, I guess October-- yeah. In September, they formed a transport of 400 Jews-- fairly healthy yet. They put us on a train, and we were riding about 5, 6 hours. And we got out of the train. We marched up into the mountains. These were the Sudeten mountains. This was on the border between Germany and Czechoslovakia. And we got up to Gross-Rosen. This was a brand new camp consisting only of five barracks. About 10 minutes before we entered the camp upstairs-- there was a huge factory. And there we were taking showers. We were dressed in new uniforms, and then we marched up to the camp. Before we entered the camp, they made-- they gave us shovels and picks. And we had to work already, before even entering the camp, till the night. And there were SS men with dogs. And they're chasing the dogs towards us. And they were beating-- beating us, and the dogs was attacking us. It was terrible. After two years, you know, you somehow get settled already. You know how to go around after two years. And here, all of a sudden, you come in a new camp, where you became a new prisoner. You have to start all over again, regardless if you had a-- a functional radio, you had better clothes, or something-- you got all the same thing. Then they took us through the camp. They put us in one barrack-- 400-- and no food. I remember there was no food. So all day, we didn't eat nothing. The next morning, we got again, coffee-- this hot water. And we marched to a stone quarry, about 25 minutes marching. And there, it started all over. They pushed people at random into the stone quarries-- killed them. We had to go march down, pick up huge rocks, putting on our shoulders, and carrying them up on the surface. And during this time, they chased us-- fast, fast, fast. And it was unbelievable, unbelievable. And all of a sudden, a foreman who was in charge of the whole groups there took my-- my friend, with whom I was running away from Katowice, with me, and gave us a special job. We remained on top of the stone quarry, on the surface. And a huge crane was downed with chains-- down and brought on a-- on a-- a lorry-- I don't how you call it. A wagon was put up. It was full of stones and brought up. And we had to unchain it and push the-- the wagon with the stones on a certain place, unload it, and bring the empty one, chain it again until it went down. So it was hard work.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we had time to relax, because we were waiting till the next one comes up. So we didn't do nothing, you know? And the SS, even, didn't do nothing to us, because we were standing and waiting. They know what we are doing, you know? And this was a hard job, but we were not beaten. We were not treated bad. One time, during lunchtime, the man-- the prisoner who operated the crane on top-- calls me. Sig, bring me some matches. And I went up and started to talk to him. And he was a fairly intelligent man. He was-- I think he was a Pole. And we talked and talked, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I forgot entirely the time is up. And I'm up there. And we're talking and talking, discussing things. And all of a sudden, I look down, and they're already assembled. They assembled all the people. They're missing one. I'm not there. And they informed already the camp to enlarge the-- the-- the borders, you know, to look for the man who is running-- who ran away. And here, I come down. And they're coming close to me, and they want to work over-- work me over, you know-- beat me up. And so the foreman comes to me, pulls me away from them, takes me in the office. And I had to put down my pants. And with the shovel steel, he hit me 25 times.

speaker: interviewee
text: And naturally, my bottom opened up, you know, bleeding. And he saved me my life. Because SS men, they would kill me, see? And he saved me my life. They couldn't intervene anymore, because they see he's working. And for weeks and weeks, I couldn't lay down on my back, only on the stomach and leg-- [LAUGHS] till it was healing up, you know? But he saved my life.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this went on-- yeah, this is what got worse and worse. We Jews, when we returned from work, it was evening. We marched back.

speaker: interviewee
text: But after the roll call, all other prisoners could go to the back, to the blocks, to barracks-- relax, get their food, and go to bed. We Jews had to remain outside and work in the camp, enlarge the camp till 12 o'clock at night. And when we came to the barrack, we didn't care anymore about the food. We just fell on our straw sacks, and we fall asleep. So we didn't eat, see?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first two months-- the first six months, we never had a drop of water to wash ourselves. They gave you soap from the beginning, but there was never water. In the morning, when we got this little container with the coffee, we left a few drops just to wet our eyes and finger-- the nails-- the-- the tips-- the fingertips. This was it. Naturally, we never changed our underwear in six months. And in no time, typhus epidemic broke out. A lot of people died. And we were getting weaker and weaker And about two years later, in October '40-- no, one year here later-- in October '42, we formed a transport of 17 people. So from those 400 who came a year before to Gross-Rosen, 17 were we-- we were alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all that remained.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all. And I have proof. I have the records here. And we were again, transported to Auschwitz. This was-- came true already. This was the worst camp of all camps. We were put on the-- on the train, and we traveling. And I looked through the window, and all the stations we are passing are looking familiar to me. And we come closer and closer. And we stopped even in my hometown, in Katowice. But they didn't let us out. Another hour, 3/4 of an hour to an hour, I look up-- Auschwitz. First of all, I never knew that Oswiecim-- the Polish name-- was changed to Auschwitz. And I-- we never knew, in three years-- we are two year-- yeah-- three years being in other camps, that there is a camp in Auschwitz. Because we didn't have no communications. We were entirely separated from the world. We didn't know from nothing-- and here, Auschwitz. We got out of the train. We marched a few minutes, because it's right there. It's the main camp-- Auschwitz-- and through the gate, and here's a camp. The camp was very familiar to me. Because since my father's family came from Auschwitz, and we, when we went to school, and my parents went three weeks of summer vacation to resorts in Germany, Czechoslovakia, and Poland, we-- my older brother and myself, we were sent to my grandmother, to Auschwitz. She lived in Auschwitz yet. And there, a little city, maybe of 10,000 to 12,000 population, not more, there were more than 90% Jewish. But I remember very well that, naturally, the non-Jews were the mayor, and the police, and-- and the-- the employees in the city. But otherwise, there were no other non-Jews, only the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very commercial city. Naturally, came Saturday, the preparation on Friday already-- quiet, you know? You-- there was no activities in business-- nothing. And only the maids who came to clean up the-- the apartments, or bringing the water from the-- the water. There was no running water in Auschwitz. There was in the-- in the middle of the market-- you have the pictures. You show it. I just can see it's there. There was a-- how you call it? It comes from the water. The water comes out of the-- of the-- of the-- Well?

speaker: interviewee
text: A well-- a water well. And from there, the non-Jews, they brought in containers to the apartments the water, you see? Yeah. And this was familiar to me. And even the camp was familiar to me, because it was a military camp. Already, I remember in Austrian time, there was this camp-- a military camp. And then we became Polish. The Pole-- the Polish military moved in. And then there came the Germans, and they turned it in-- into a military camp-- into a concentration camp. And this Auschwitz was the only concentration camp of all 1,500 camps in occupied Europe which was not built by the Germans. Because this was existing already. It was a two-story red building-- buildings. You know it. You were there already. So it was familiar to me. We came there. Right away, the first thing, they tattooed the numbers. First of all, they made selections. They select the old men, the sick men, right now, separated. And we-- we remained-- I would say about 800.

speaker: interviewee
text: But not only we came from Germany, from the concentration camp. But from all the remaining German concentration camp, all Jews were brought to Auschwitz in order to liquidated. This was after the Final Solution. Yeah? And-- What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this that you came?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- this-- the Final Solution was in '41. In November, they assembled near Berlin. And one day, the high officials-- and they decided, we will get rid of all the Jews from the camps. And that's the reason they liquidated all German camps from Jews-- from the Jews. And they brought us to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you came in '41 to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came-- no. I came in '42, in October, you see? And all other ones-- we were the first ones from Gross-Rosen. And then came Freddy Diamond, from Sachsenhausen, and all other people from other camps. And we were distributed in different camps. And we, at night, after 10 days being in Auschwitz, doing nothing, we formed a transport with about 800. It fall down again.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
